original_abstract: We present a self-propelled colloidal hematite "docker" capable of navigating toward, docking with, and transporting cargo particles significantly larger than itself to remote destinations. This entire sequence—incorporating propulsion, docking, and release—is reversible and controlled externally via visible light. Guidance of the docker is achieved through either weak uniform magnetic fields or physical nanoscale tracks on a textured substrate. The underlying mechanism for both motion and docking stems from osmotic and phoretic transport driven by concentration gradients of hydrogen peroxide, which are generated by the hematite's photocatalytic activity. This docking strategy is highly versatile, accommodating a wide range of cargo materials and geometries. Furthermore, these dockers are simple, single-component particles that can be synthesized in bulk quantities. This platform provides a robust foundation for the development of sophisticated micrometer-scale factories and advanced biomimetic systems.
humanized_abstract: Here, we demonstrate the self-driven movement of a colloidal hematite “docker” towards cargo particles, to dock with them and then transport them to arbitrary release locations. The whole process (which includes propulsion, docking and cargo release) can be done reversibly and under remote control using simply visible light. Guidance of the “docker” is either done by a weak uniform magnetic field or by tracks of a few nanometres width engraved in a textured substrate surface. The motion and docking behaviour is due to an osmotic/phoretic propulsion resulting from gradients in hydrogen peroxide concentration generated around the photocatalytic hematite. The strategy is highly versatile as regards the nature and shape of the cargoes. Furthermore, the system we introduce consists of simple single component micrometer-sized particles that can be produced in large quantities. In summary, these “docker” particles open the way towards the creation of complex micrometer scale “factories” as well as to highly advanced bio-mimicking functionalities.

The strategy is highly versatile as regards the nature and shape of the cargoes. In summary, these “docker” particles open the way towards the creation of complex micrometer scale “factories” as well as to highly advanced bio-mimicking functionalities.
